title: Magnetic Effect of Current Test - 3 (Topic Test)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: Escape speed from the Earth is close to 11.2 km s⁻¹. On another planet whose radius is half of the Earth's radius and whose mass density is four times that of the Earth, the escape speed in km s⁻¹ will be close to :
questions.solution:
To find the escape speed from another planet given its radius and mass density in relation to Earth's, we need to use the concept of escape velocity. The escape velocity $ v_e $ from a spherical body is given by:

$$ v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}} $$

where:

  • $ G $ is the gravitational constant
  • $ M $ is the mass of the planet
  • $ R $ is the radius of the planet

We know the escape speed from Earth is 11.2 km/s. Therefore:

$$ v_{e, \text{Earth}} = 11.2 \text{ km/s} $$

We denote the escape velocity of the new planet as $ v_{e, \text{planet}} $. Let's express the mass of the new planet in terms of Earth's mass.

The mass $ M $ of a planet can be written as the product of its volume and density:

$$ M = \rho V $$

For a spherical body:

$$ V = \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 $$

Therefore:

$$ M \propto \rho R^3 $$

Given the new planet's radius $ R_p $ is half of Earth's radius $ R_e $:

$$ R_p = \frac{R_e}{2} $$

Also, its density $ \rho_p $ is four times the density of Earth $ \rho_e $:

$$ \rho_p = 4 \rho_e $$

The new planet's mass $ M_p $ compared to Earth's mass $ M_e $ can be written as:

$$ M_p \propto \rho_p R_p^3 = 4 \rho_e \left(\frac{R_e}{2}\right)^3 = 4 \rho_e \left(\frac{R_e^3}{8}\right) = \frac{\rho_e R_e^3}{2} = \frac{M_e}{2} $$

Thus:

$$ M_p = \frac{M_e}{2} $$

Now we substitute $ M_p $ and $ R_p $ into the escape velocity formula:

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M_p}{R_p}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G \frac{M_e}{2}}{\frac{R_e}{2}}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 G M_e}{R_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{G M_e}{R_e}} $$

This is because the factor of 2 in the denominator of the mass term and the factor of 2 in the radius term cancels out. So:

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = \sqrt{2} v_{e, \text{Earth}} $$

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = \sqrt{2} \cdot 11.2 \text{ km/s} $$

Calculating this value:

$$ v_{e, \text{planet}} = 11.2 \sqrt{2} \approx 11.2 \cdot 1.414 = 15.8 \text{ km/s} $$

Thus, the correct option is:\p>

Option B

15.8

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is true?
options: The force of gravity of the Earth on the Moon is greater than the force of gravity of the Moon on the Earth.
The force of gravity of the Moon on the Earth is greater than the force of gravity of the Earth on the Moon.
The force of gravity of the Earth on the Moon and of the Moon on the Earth are equal in magnitude and are in the same direction.
The force of gravity of the Earth on the Moon and of the Moon on the Earth are equal in magnitude but are in opposite directions.
questions.solution:
To determine the correct answer, we need to apply Newton's Third Law of Motion. Newton's Third Law states that for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. This means the forces two objects exert on each other are equal in magnitude but opposite in direction.

In the case of the Earth and the Moon, the gravitational force that the Earth exerts on the Moon is equal in magnitude to the gravitational force that the Moon exerts on the Earth. However, these forces are in opposite directions.

Mathematically, the force due to gravity between two masses, $m_1$ (Earth) and $m_2$ (Moon), is given by Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation:

$$ F = G \frac{m_1 m_2}{r^2} $$

where $G$ is the gravitational constant, and $r$ is the distance between the centers of the two masses.

Since the masses and the distance between the centers remain the same, the magnitude of the force is the same for both the Earth and the Moon. Hence, by Newton's Third Law, we know that these forces are also equal in magnitude and opposite in direction.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option D: The force of gravity of the Earth on the Moon and of the Moon on the Earth are equal in magnitude but are in opposite directions.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: There is a ball of mass 320 g. It has 625 J potential energy when released freely from a height. The speed with which it will hit the ground is
questions.solution:
First convert the mass to kilograms:

m = 320 g = 0.32 kg

All the potential energy turns into kinetic energy at the bottom:

$$\tfrac12\,m\,v^2 = \text{PE} = 625\text{ J}$$

Solving for v:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2\cdot625}{0.32}} = \sqrt{3906.25} = 62.5\text{ m/s}$$

So the ball hits the ground at 62.5 m/s (Option 1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is true for sound waves propa- gating in air?
options: Sound is an electromagnetic wave and transverse in nature
Sound is a mechanical wave and longitudinal in nature
Sound is a mechanical wave and transverse in nature
Sound is an electromagnetic wave and longitudinal in nature
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option B: Sound is a mechanical wave and longitudinal in nature.

Here's why:

Mechanical Waves:

  • Mechanical waves require a medium to travel. They are disturbances that travel through a medium by transferring energy from one particle to the next.
  • Sound waves are mechanical because they need air, water, solids, or any other medium to propagate.

Longitudinal Waves:

  • In longitudinal waves, the particles of the medium vibrate parallel to the direction of wave propagation. This means that the particles move back and forth in the same direction that the wave is traveling.
  • Sound waves are longitudinal because the air molecules vibrate back and forth in the same direction that the sound wave is traveling.

Why other options are incorrect:

  • Option A and D: Electromagnetic waves do not require a medium to travel. They can travel through a vacuum, like space. Examples of electromagnetic waves include light, radio waves, and X-rays.
  • Option C: Transverse waves are waves where the particles of the medium vibrate perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. An example of a transverse wave is a wave on a string.

In summary, sound waves are mechanical waves that travel through a medium and are longitudinal because the particles of the medium vibrate parallel to the direction of wave propagation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A mass is attached to a spring that hangs vertically. The extension produced in the spring is 6 cm on Earth. The acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the Moon is one-sixth of its value on the surface of the Earth. The extension of the spring on the Moon would be:
questions.solution:
A spring that obeys Hooke's Law extends by a distance proportional to the force applied to it. When a mass $ m $ is attached to a vertical spring, the force applied to the spring is the weight of the mass, which can be expressed as $ F = mg $, where $ g $ is the acceleration due to gravity.

Given that the extension produced in the spring on Earth is 6 cm (or 0.06 m), we can write this as:

$$ F_\text{Earth} = k \cdot \Delta x_\text{Earth} $$

where $ k $ is the spring constant and $ \Delta x_\text{Earth} $ is the extension on Earth. Rearranging this equation, we get:

$$ mg = k \cdot 0.06 $$

Since we are comparing the extension on the Moon to the Earth, we need to consider the change in the gravitational force. On the Moon, the acceleration due to gravity $ g_\text{Moon} $ is one-sixth of that on Earth:

$$ g_\text{Moon} = \frac{g}{6} $$

The force exerted by the mass on the Moon would then be:

$$ F_\text{Moon} = mg_\text{Moon} = m \cdot \frac{g}{6} $$

This force causes a different extension in the spring, which we will denote as $ \Delta x_\text{Moon} $. Applying Hooke’s Law on the Moon, we have:

$$ F_\text{Moon} = k \cdot \Delta x_\text{Moon} $$

Substituting for $ F_\text{Moon} $ from earlier, we get:

$$ m \cdot \frac{g}{6} = k \cdot \Delta x_\text{Moon} $$

Since we know from the Earth’s situation that $ mg = k \cdot 0.06 $, we can substitute $ mg $ into the equation:

$$ \frac{k \cdot 0.06}{6} = k \cdot \Delta x_\text{Moon} $$

Simplifying this, we find:

$$ \Delta x_\text{Moon} = \frac{0.06}{6} = 0.01 \, \text{m} $$

Therefore, the extension of the spring on the Moon would be 1 cm. So, the correct answer is:

Option B: 1 cm

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: The length of a simple pendulum is increased four times to its previous value while the mass is doubled. What is the ratio of the new and previous time period of the pendulum?
questions.solution:
Calculation:

The time period of a simple pendulum is given by the formula:

$ T_{\text{old}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} $

If the length $ L $ of the pendulum is increased four times and the mass is doubled, we note that the time period is independent of the pendulum's mass. Therefore, the time period depends only on the length and gravitational acceleration.

For the new pendulum:

$ T_{\text{new}} = 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{4L}{g}} $

Simplifying this expression:

$ T_{\text{new}} = 2\pi\sqrt{4}\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} $

$ T_{\text{new}} = 2 \times 2\pi\sqrt{\frac{L}{g}} $

Thus, comparing the initial and new time periods:

$ T_{\text{new}} = 2T_{\text{old}} $

The ratio of the new to the old time period is:

$ T_{\text{new}} : T_{\text{old}} = 2 : 1 $

Therefore, the ratio of the new and previous time periods is 2:1.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText:
Given below are the four cases in which certain heat transfer is taking place:

1. Ice is melting in a glass full of water

2. Water is boiling in an open container

3. A metal rod is heated in a furnace

4. A cup of coffee is allowed to cool on a table

In which of the above cases, the Newton's Law of Cooling is applicable ?
questions.solution:
Newton’s law of cooling applies when a body (with uniform temperature) exchanges heat by convection (or convection + radiation) with its surroundings, without any phase change. In formula form:

$$\frac{dT}{dt}=-k\bigl(T(t)-T_\infty\bigr)$$

where

• $T(t)$ is the object’s temperature at time $t$,

• $T_\infty$ is the ambient temperature,

• $k$ is a positive constant (proportional to the heat‐transfer coefficient).

Checking each case:

Ice melting in water

– Phase change at constant $0^\circ$C → temperature doesn’t change, so the simple $dT/dt$ law doesn’t apply.

Water boiling in an open container

– Phase change at $100^\circ$C → again constant temperature during boiling.

Metal rod heated in a furnace

– Often involves conduction along the rod and radiation at high temperatures; temperature is not uniform and there’s more than simple convection.

Cup of coffee cooling on a table

– No phase change, coffee cools by convection/radiation, roughly uniform temperature → fits Newton’s law.

Answer: Only case 4.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: Two identical containers X and Y are connected at the bottom by a thin tube of negligible volume. The tube has a valve in it, as shown in the figure. Initially container X has a liquid filled up to height h in it and container Y is empty. When the valve is opened, both containers have equal amount of liquid in equilibrium. If the initial (before the valve is opened) potential energy of the liquid is P1 and the final potential energy is P2 then :
options: P1 = 4P2
P1 = 2P2
P1 = 8P2
questions.solution:
1. Let the cross-sectional area of each container be $A$ and the liquid density be $\rho$. Total mass of liquid initially in $X$ is

    $$m = \rho A h.$$

  2. Choose the bottom of the containers as the reference level ($y=0$).

    – Initial centre of mass of liquid in $X$ is at height $h/2$.

    – Initial potential energy

    $$P_1 = m g \,\frac{h}{2} = (\rho A h)\,g\,\frac{h}{2} = \frac{\rho g A h^2}{2}.$$

  3. After opening the valve, the liquid distributes equally: each container has height $h/2$.

    – Mass in each container is $\rho A (h/2)$.

    – Centre of mass in each is at half that height, $\,\tfrac{h/2}{2}=h/4\,$.

    – Potential energy of liquid in one container:

    $$P_{\rm one} = \Bigl[\rho A \tfrac{h}{2}\Bigr] g \,\frac{h}{4} = \frac{\rho g A h^2}{8}.$$

    – Total final potential energy in both containers:

    $$P_2 = 2\,P_{\rm one} = 2\cdot\frac{\rho g A h^2}{8} = \frac{\rho g A h^2}{4}.$$

  4. Ratio

    $$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \frac{\tfrac{\rho g A h^2}{2}}{\tfrac{\rho g A h^2}{4}} = \frac12\div\frac14 = 2 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad P_1 = 2\,P_2.$$

Answer: Option C.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is not correct for light rays?
options: Light travels at different speeds in different media
Light travels at almost 300 million metres per second in air
Light speeds down as it leaves a water surface and enters the air
Light speeds up as it leaves a glass surface and enters the air
questions.solution:
The statement that is not correct is Option C: Light speeds down as it leaves a water surface and enters the air.

Here's why:

Light travels at different speeds in different media because the refractive index of each medium is different. The refractive index (n) is a measure of how much a medium slows down light compared to its speed in a vacuum ($c \approx 3 \times 10^8 \, m/s$). The speed of light in a medium ($v$) is given by:

$$ v = \frac{c}{n} $$

When light travels from a denser medium (like water) to a rarer medium (like air), its speed increases. This is because the refractive index of water is greater than that of air. The phenomenon of light changing speed and bending at the boundary between two media is called refraction. Snell's Law describes the relationship between the angles of incidence and refraction and the refractive indices of the two media:

$$ n_1 \sin(\theta_1) = n_2 \sin(\theta_2) $$

where:

$n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices of the first and second media, respectively.

$\theta_1$ is the angle of incidence.

$\theta_2$ is the angle of refraction.

Therefore, as light leaves water and enters air, its speed increases (it speeds up), not decreases (slows down). Option D correctly reflects this behavior. Option A is true, as speed depends on the refractive index of the medium. Option B is approximately true; light's speed in air is very close to its speed in a vacuum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is the lowest possible temperature?
questions.solution:
The lowest possible temperature among the given options is Option D: $-273$° Celsius. This temperature is equivalent to absolute zero, which is 0 Kelvin ($0 \, \text{K}$). Absolute zero is the theoretical temperature at which substances contain no thermal energy and particles are at their minimum motion. It marks the complete absence of heat energy.

In the Celsius scale, absolute zero is expressed as $-273.15$° Celsius. Thus, any temperature lower numerically like $-273$° Celsius, symbolically represents this concept of absolute zero. No temperature can be lower than absolute zero as it is the absolute limit of thermal motion in particle physics.

Therefore, in the context of the given options, $-273$° Celsius is the representation of the theoretical minimum temperature possible.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: The power required to lift a mass of 8⋅0 kg up a vertical distance of 4 m in 2 s is (taking acceleration due to gravity as 10 m/s<sup>2</sup>):
questions.solution:
To determine the power required, we need to follow these steps:

Calculate the work done to lift the mass.

Calculate the power using the time in which the work is done.

Step 1: Calculate the Work Done

Work done $ W $ is given by:

$ W = m \cdot g \cdot h $

where:

$ m $ is the mass ($ 8.0 \, \text{kg} $),

$ g $ is the acceleration due to gravity ($ 10 \, \text{m/s}^2 $),

$ h $ is the height ($ 4 \, \text{m} $).

So,

$ W = 8.0 \, \text{kg} \times 10 \, \text{m/s}^2 \times 4 \, \text{m} $

$ W = 320 \, \text{J} $

Step 2: Calculate the Power

Power $ P $ is given by:

$ P = \frac{W}{t} $

where $ t $ is the time ($ 2 \, \text{s} $).

So,

$ P = \frac{320 \, \text{J}}{2 \, \text{s}} $

$ P = 160 \, \text{W} $

Conclusion

The power required to lift the mass is $ 160 \, \text{W} $. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option B: 160 W

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: Which one among the following diagrams may correctly represent the motion of a skydiver during a jump ?
questions.solution:
CONCEPT:

How a Skydiver Falls

Step 1: Skydiver Jumps

When a skydiver jumps out, gravity pulls them down. Their speed goes up fast at first because they are falling freely through the air.

Step 2: Reaching Terminal Velocity

As the skydiver falls faster, air pushes back harder. Eventually, the push from the air (air resistance) matches the pull of gravity, so the skydiver's speed stops increasing. This steady speed is called terminal velocity.

Step 3: Opening the Parachute

When the parachute opens, there is a sudden big increase in air resistance. This makes the skydiver slow down very quickly.

Speed-Time Graph:

The speed-time graph starts by going up quickly (as the skydiver speeds up). Then, it flattens out (terminal velocity). After the parachute opens, the line drops sharply as the skydiver slows down fast.

EXPLANATION:

Option 1:

This graph is correct. It shows speed going up fast, staying the same for a while (terminal velocity), and then dropping quickly after the parachute opens.

Option 2:

This option is incorrect because it makes the speed go to zero too fast, which doesn't happen in real skydiving.

Option 3:

This option is wrong because it shows speed going up and down in a straight line, as if the skydiver stops suddenly without a parachute. This is unrealistic.

Option 4:

This option almost matches what really happens, but it does not clearly show a flat part for terminal velocity and the slowing down isn't sudden enough when the parachute opens.

So, Option 1 is correct.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: What is the mass of a material, whose specific heat capacity is 400 J/(kg °C) for a rise in temperature from 15°C to 25°C, when heat received is 20 kJ ?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: A positive charge is moving towards south in a space where magnetic field is pointing in the north direction. The moving charge will experience :
options: a deflecting force towards north direction.
a deflecting force towards east direction.
a deflecting force towards west direction.
questions.solution:
The correct answer is Option D (no deflecting force). Here's why:

Understanding the Concept:

Magnetic Force on a Moving Charge: A charged particle moving in a magnetic field experiences a force only if its velocity has a component perpendicular to the magnetic field direction.

Right-Hand Rule: You can determine the direction of the magnetic force using the right-hand rule. However, in this scenario, we don't need to apply the rule.

Explanation:

Since the positive charge is moving parallel (towards South) to the magnetic field (pointing North), there is no perpendicular component of velocity.

In simpler terms, the charge is moving "along" the magnetic field lines, not "across" them. Therefore, it experiences no magnetic force to deflect it.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.questionText: The magnetic field produced by a current-carrying straight wire at a point outside the wire depends
options: inversely on the distance from it
directly on the distance from it
inversely at short distances and directly at large distances from it
directly on the distance (at short distances) and inversely on the distance (at long distances) from it
